original_abstract: Developing efficient solar-to-hydrogen conversion technologies depends heavily on the discovery of high-performance catalysts for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). In this study, we investigate the mechanisms behind the exceptional performance of mixed nickel-iron oxyhydroxides (Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH), which exhibit a 500-fold increase in OER activity compared to their pure nickel and iron counterparts—positioning them among the most active OER catalysts known in alkaline media. Using operando high-energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) X-ray absorption spectroscopy, we show that Fe³⁺ ions in the mixed lattice occupy octahedral sites characterized by unusually short Fe–O bond distances. This unique coordination environment is driven by edge-sharing with the surrounding [NiO₆] framework. Through density functional theory calculations, we demonstrate that this structural motif optimizes the adsorption energies of OER intermediates at the iron sites, significantly lowering the overpotential. Conversely, our findings indicate that the nickel sites remain spectator species and do not serve as active centers for water oxidation.
humanized_abstract: Discovering catalysts that facilitate the solar-to-hydrogen conversion at water-splitting efficiency remains an open and essential challenge. A key catalyst that must be identified is a highly efficient catalyst for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Here, a series of mixed nickel-iron oxyhydroxides catalysts Ni₁₋ₓFeₓOOH are synthesized. A 500-fold increase in catalytic activity is realized in comparison with pure nickel and iron oxides—affording one of the most active catalysts reported in alkaline solution for the OER reaction. The catalytic sites and edge structures of the highly active NiFeOOH are elucidated at the operating condition using operando high-energy resolution fluorescence detection (HERFD) X-ray absorption spectroscopy. It is found that Fe ions located in the octahedral site have shorter Fe–O distances and the Fe3+ ions at such sites are likely to be on the edge-sharing sites of [NiO₆] octahedra. Further supported by the density functional theory calculations, optimal adsorption energies of oxygen evolution reaction intermediates are found at iron active sites, thus reducing the catalyst overpotential. The nickel sites serve only as a structure to hold on the iron active sites and play a passive role in the catalytic process.
